risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Randomization was used for allocation of NPWT and fixation strip to the right or left breast incision site per patient, using sealed envelopes. Treatment site information was accessed digitally (www.sealedenvelope.com) upon the start of the treatment postsurgically." Comment: Although the paper does not spell out the exact sequence-generation algorithm, the use of the Sealed Envelope randomization service and randomized allocation to right/left breast implies a proper random allocation sequence rather than a predictable rule, so Low risk is appropriate.

Quote: "Randomization was used for allocation of NPWT and fixation strip to the right or left breast incision site per patient, using sealed envelopes. Treatment site information was accessed digitally (www.sealedenvelope.com) upon the start of the treatment postsurgically." Comment: This explicitly describes a concealment mechanism (sealed envelopes with digital access at treatment start) that prevented foreknowledge of the next assignment before enrollment/assignment, so allocation concealment is judged low risk.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "As NPWT and fixation strips are optically different, blinding of the physician and patients was not feasible" and "investigator and patient bias scoring POSAS and VAS cannot be ruled out." Comment: Participants and treating personnel were explicitly not blinded because the two treatments looked different, and the outcomes included wound healing complications and scar quality/aesthetic assessments that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation. Across the study outcomes overall, this indicates a high risk of performance bias.

Quote: "Clinicians assessed the surgical site condition for wound healing status at 21 days, scoring on a clinical scale." Quote: "The patient and observer also scored their 'overall opinion'" and "Given the nature of the treatments, it was not possible to conduct a double-blind trial... investigator and patient bias scoring POSAS and VAS cannot be ruled out... the assessor was not available. Instead, the investigator did not know the randomization schedule and the patients were asked not to reveal it." Comment: The key outcomes included clinician-assessed wound healing and observer-rated scar quality/VAS, which are subjective/judgement-based and could be influenced by knowledge of allocation. The paper explicitly states blinding of the assessor was unsuccessful, so detection bias is plausible.

Quote: "All included patients (N = 32) had follow-up visits and assessments at screening (pre-surgery), day 0 (baseline, post-surgery), day 7, 21, 42, 90, 180 and 365 days post-surgery." Comment: The report explicitly states that all 32 included patients completed the scheduled follow-up assessments through 365 days, with no indication of missing outcome data, withdrawals, or exclusions after randomization. This supports a Low risk judgement for attrition bias.
